---
title: "Client Report - Highest Paid Positions"
subtitle: "Unit 2 Task 4"
author: "Camila Rojas"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd 
import numpy as np
import sqlite3
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
sqlite_file = 'lahmansbaseballdb.sqlite'
# this file must be in the same location as your .qmd or .py file
con = sqlite3.connect(sqlite_file)
```


## Question 1

__Write an SQL query that provides a summary table showing the average salary for each position (e.g., pitcher, catcher, outfielder). Position information can be found in the fielding table in the POS column.__

Include the following columns:

- position
- average_salary
- total_players
- highest_salary
The highest_salary column should display the highest salary ever earned by a player in that position.

Additionally, create a new column called salary_category using a case statement:

If the average salary is above $3 million, categorize it as “High Salary.”
If the average salary is between $2 million and $3 million, categorize it as “Medium Salary.”
Otherwise, categorize it as “Low Salary.”
Order the table by average salary in descending order.

From my results, 1B has the highest average salary at about $3.34M (High Salary) with 472 total players. OF is next at about $2.41M (Medium Salary) and has 1121 players, so a lot more players than 1B. C has the lowest average salary at about $1.44M (Low Salary). The highest_salary column shows that some positions can still have very large single salaries even when their overall average is lower.

```{python}

q = """

    WITH max_games AS (
        SELECT playerID,
            yearID,
            MAX(G) AS maxG
        FROM fielding
        GROUP BY playerID, yearID
    ),
    primary_pos AS (
        SELECT f.playerID,
            f.yearID,
            MIN(f.POS) AS position
        FROM fielding AS f
        JOIN max_games AS m
        ON f.playerID = m.playerID
        AND f.yearID  = m.yearID
        AND f.G       = m.maxG
        GROUP BY f.playerID, f.yearID
    ),
    salary_by_player_year AS (
        SELECT playerID,
            yearID,
            SUM(salary) AS salary
        FROM salaries
        GROUP BY playerID, yearID
    )
    SELECT p.position,
        ROUND(AVG(s.salary), 2) AS average_salary,
        COUNT(DISTINCT p.playerID) AS total_players,
        MAX(s.salary) AS highest_salary,
        CASE
            WHEN AVG(s.salary) > 3000000 THEN 'High Salary'
            WHEN AVG(s.salary) BETWEEN 2000000 AND 3000000 THEN 'Medium Salary'
            ELSE 'Low Salary'
        END AS salary_category
    FROM primary_pos AS p
    JOIN salary_by_player_year AS s
    ON p.playerID = s.playerID
    AND p.yearID  = s.yearID
    GROUP BY p.position
    ORDER BY average_salary DESC;

"""
pos_results = pd.read_sql_query(q, con)
pos_results

```
